project.title: Proteomics of groundwater microbiomes collected from five wells in Alberta, Canada
project.projectDescription: Groundwater samples were collected from five wells in Alberta, Canada. The sampling location and time are indicated in the table below. 13 to 29 genomes were assembled from each metagenome. Proteomic analyses were performed to investigate which genomes and genes were expressed in each well. Sample ID Location Latitude (NAD 83) Longitude (NAD 83) Sampling date 19GWC19026 218 Cluny 50.85 -112.84 30/07/2019 19GWC19028 114 Ross Creek 49.99 -110.46 31/07/2019 19GWC19045 265 Metiskow 52.42 -110.61 18/09/2019 19GWE00050 991 Cynthia 56.22 -117.81 04/09/2019 19GWE00515 33 Fort McMurray 56.98 -111.85 17/09/2019
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: For protein extraction, 1 - 20 L samples were filtered through 0.10 ¼m pore size membrane disc filters (Millipore Sigma). The membranes were cut and transferred to lysing matrix bead tubes A (MP Biomedicals) with the addition of SDT-lysis buffer (0.1M DTT) in a 10:1 ratio. Matrix tubes were bead-beated in an OMNI Bead Ruptor 24 for 45 s at 6 m s1 and then incubated at 95 °C for 10 min. Peptides were isolated from pellets by filter-aided sample preparation (FASP) (Wisniewski et al., 2009, Nat. Meth. 6(5)). A Qubit 2.0 Fluorometer (Invitrogen, CA) was used to check protein concentrations.  Samples were analyzed by 1D-LC-MS/MS. Two wash runs and one blank run were done between samples to reduce carry over. For each run, 2000 ng of peptide solution were loaded onto a 5 mm, 300 µm ID C18 Acclaim® PepMap100 pre-column (Thermo Fisher Scientific) using an UltiMateTM 3000 RSLCnano Liquid Chromatograph (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and desalted on the pre-column. After desalting the peptides, the pre-column was switched in line with a 75 cm x 75 µm analytical EASY-Spray column packed with PepMap RSLC C18, 2 µm material (Thermo Fisher Scientific), which was heated to 60 °C. The analytical column was connected via an Easy-Spray source to a Q Exactive Plus hybrid quadrupole-Orbitrap mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Peptides were separated on the analytical column using a 460 min gradient as described by Kleiner et al. (2017, Nat. Comm. 8(1558)) and mass spectra were acquired in the Orbitrap as described by Petersen et al. (2016, Nat. Microb. 2(16195)). 270,275 to 312,887 MS/MS spectra were acquired per sample.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: For protein identification, the database of a sample was created using predicted protein sequences of all binned and unbinned contigs from the corresponding metagenome. Redundant proteins (>95% amino acid identity) in each database were removed by cd-hit (Li et al., 2006, Bioinformatics, 22(13)), while giving preference to proteins from binned contigs. The cRAP protein sequence database (http://www.thegpm.org/crap/) containing protein sequences of common laboratory contaminants was appended to the database. The final database of each sample contained 66,904 to 129,486 protein sequences. For protein identification MS/MS spectra were searched against the database using the Sequest HT node in Proteome Discoverer version 2.2.0.388 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, CA, USA). The Percolator Node and FidoCT were used to estimate false discovery rates (FDR) at the peptide and protein level, respectively. Only proteins identified with medium or high confidence were retained, resulting in an overall false discovery rate of < 5%. Relative abundance of proteins was estimated based on normalized peptide-spectral matches (PSMs).

references.referenceLine: Li S, Mosier D, Kouris A, Humez P, Mayer B, Strous M, Diao M. High diversity, abundance, and expression of hydrogenases in groundwater. ISME Commun. 2024 4(1):ycae023
